words.g: the a prophet or inspired man

verses.flow: The burden (massa') which Habakkuk the prophet did see.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Massa'" (Strong's H4853) means "burden" or "oracle," often used for weighty prophetic pronouncements, especially those involving judgment (cf. Isa 13:1; Nah 1:1). The term comes from nasa', "to lift up," suggesting a word so heavy it must be carried. Habakkuk's name may derive from "habaq," meaning "to embrace," though this is disputed.

verses.flow: How long, O YHWH, have I cried for help, and you do not hear? I cry out to you, "Violence (hamas)!" and you do not save.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Habakkuk's complaint is a formal lament (cf. Ps 22; Jer 20:7-18), a recognized genre of Israelite prayer in which the prophet boldly questions YHWH's apparent inaction. This is not faithlessness but covenantal dialogue — the prophet holds YHWH to his own covenant obligations. The book opens with theodicy at its sharpest.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Why do you make me see iniquity, and why do you look upon trouble (amal)? Destruction and violence (hamas) are before me; strife arises, and contention lifts itself up.
Therefore the law (torah) is paralyzed, and justice (mishpat) never goes forth. For the wicked surround the righteous; therefore justice (mishpat) goes forth perverted.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Torah" (H8451) here refers to the Mosaic Law as the covenant framework for social justice. "Mishpat" (H4941) denotes justice in a legal, covenantal sense — both the act of judgment and its outcome. The verb "go forth perverted" (yetse' mequqqal) pictures justice emerging twisted, distorted at its source.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc plur
conjunction + verb hithpael imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: Look among the nations, and see; wonder and be astounded. For a work is being worked in your days that you would not believe if it were told.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Paul cites this verse (LXX form) in Acts 13:41 as a warning to those who would reject the gospel message regarding the resurrection of Jesus, applying the "work in your days" to the new-covenant act of God in Christ. The verse thus carries a double fulfillment: the immediate Babylonian invasion and the eschatological work of salvation.

words.m: conjunction + particle + verb niphal participle active

verses.flow: For behold, I am raising up the Chaldeans (Kasdim), that bitter and hasty nation, who march through the breadth of the earth to seize dwellings not their own.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The Chaldeans (Kasdim, H3778) were a Semitic people who came to dominate Babylon under Nabopolassar and Nebuchadnezzar II (c. 626–562 BC). Their rise to power and brutal military campaigns against Judah (605, 597, 586 BC) are extensively documented in the Babylonian Chronicles and corroborated by archaeology. DIVINE_COUNCIL: YHWH's declaration "I am raising up" (hineni meqim) asserts his sovereignty over the movement of pagan empires, a key tenet of the divine council worldview in which YHWH governs the nations (Deut 32:8; Ps 82).

words.g: and an elevation or leprous scab

verses.flow: Dreadful and fearsome is he; his justice (mishpat) and his dignity (se'et) go forth from himself.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd common plur

words.g: and to be sharp or severe
words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd common plur
conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: His horses are swifter than leopards, and keener than wolves of the evening; his horsemen press forward, and his horsemen come from afar — they fly as an eagle hastening to devour.

words.g: and to gather for any purpose
words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: All of them come for violence (hamas); the entirety of their faces presses forward; he gathers captives like sand.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: He himself scoffs at kings, and rulers are a laughingstock to him; he laughs at every fortress, and heaps up earth and captures it.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing

words.g: to/for gods in the ordinary sense

verses.flow: Then he sweeps past as a wind and goes on — and he becomes guilty, even this one whose strength is his god.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The clause "whose strength is his god" is a devastating indictment of Babylonian imperial theology, in which military power was attributed to Marduk and the divine sanction of the king. Habakkuk reframes this as idolatry — the empire worships its own might. This anticipates the oracle of Hab 2:18-19 against idol-makers.

words.m: verb qal perfect 2nd masc sing + suffix
verb qal perfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Are you not from everlasting, O YHWH, my God, my Holy One? We shall not die. O YHWH, you have appointed him for judgment; and O Rock (Tsur), you have established him for correction.
verses.note: HEBREW: "From everlasting" (miqqedem) affirms YHWH's eternal pre-existence, contrasted with the temporal rise of Babylon. "Rock" (Tsur, H6697) is a divine epithet rooted in Mosaic poetry (Deut 32:4, 15, 18, 30-31), carrying connotations of immovable faithfulness and covenant reliability. The phrase "We shall not die" was altered by scribal tradition (tikkun sopherim) from an original "You shall not die" — a deliberate emendation to avoid apparent irreverence toward YHWH.

verses.flow: You who are of eyes too pure to see evil, and who cannot look upon trouble (amal) — why do you look upon those who deal treacherously? Why are you silent when the wicked swallows up the man more righteous than he?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse presents the central tension of the entire book: YHWH's moral purity (he cannot look upon evil) stands in apparent contradiction to his using an even more wicked instrument (Babylon) to punish his own people. This is the problem of theodicy — how can a holy God employ unholy means? The resolution comes in chapter 2 with the vision of divine judgment upon Babylon itself.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 2nd masc sing

words.g: the a sea or large body of water

verses.flow: You have made man like the fish of the sea, like crawling things having no ruler over them.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

words.g: and to gather for any purpose
words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: He brings all of them up with a hook; he drags them out with his net; he gathers them in his dragnet. Therefore he rejoices and exults.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Therefore he sacrifices to his net and makes offerings to his dragnet, for by them his portion is fat and his food is rich.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The imagery of Babylonian soldiers sacrificing to their weapons and military equipment is not merely metaphorical — ANE texts document rituals honoring weapons and battle standards as extensions of divine power. Herodotus records similar practices among conquering powers. The net and dragnet imagery may reflect Assyrian and Babylonian bas-reliefs depicting conquered peoples literally netted like fish.

words.g: to/for to smite with deadly intent

verses.flow: Shall he therefore empty his net, and shall he not spare to slay nations continually?

words.m: conjunction + verb hithpael cohortative 1st common sing
conjunction + verb piel imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: I will stand at my watch-post and station myself on the tower, and I will look out to see what he will speak in me, and what I shall answer concerning my complaint.
verses.note: HEBREW: "watch-post" (mishmeret) conveys a military sentinel's station; the prophet positions himself as a watchman awaiting divine reply. "What I shall answer concerning my complaint" reflects the Hebrew tokachat, meaning a rebuke or reproof — Habakkuk anticipates that YHWH may rebuke him for his bold questioning (Hab 1:12-17). The posture is one of expectant, disciplined waiting.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb piel imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Then YHWH answered me and said: "Write the vision; make it plain upon tablets, so that one may run who reads it.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: For still the vision awaits its appointed time (moed); it hastens to the end — it will not lie. If it seems slow, wait for it; it will surely come, it will not delay.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "appointed time" (moed, H4150) is the same term used for Israel's sacred festivals — creation-embedded markers of divine appointment. The declaration "it will surely come, it will not delay" is the OT anchor for NT eschatological patience (2 Pet 3:9). TYPOLOGY: The early church and the author of Hebrews (10:37) read this verse messianically, identifying the "one who is coming" with Christ himself, whose parousia is certain though delayed in human perception.

verses.flow: Behold, his soul is puffed up; it is not upright within him. But the righteous shall live by his faithfulness (emunah).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Faithfulness" (emunah, H530) denotes firmness, steadiness, reliability — not merely intellectual belief but covenantal fidelity and trust. This verse is the most-quoted OT verse in the NT epistles: Paul cites it in Rom 1:17 and Gal 3:11 to establish justification by faith, while Hebrews 10:38 uses it to exhort perseverance under persecution. The 1QpHab Qumran pesher interprets "the righteous" as those who observe the Torah and trust the Teacher of Righteousness. STRUCTURE: The verse creates a sharp antithesis between the proud/puffed-up soul (the Chaldean oppressor) and the righteous who endure by emunah — establishing the book's central theological tension.

words.g: as/like Hades or the world of the dead
and to gather for any purpose
words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Moreover, wine is a treacherous one — a proud man who does not remain at home. He enlarges his appetite like Sheol, and like death he never has enough. He gathers to himself all nations, and collects to himself all peoples.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Shall not all these take up a taunt (mashal) against him — a mocking riddle — and say: "Woe to him who heaps up what is not his own! How long? And who loads himself down with pledges!"
verses.note: HEBREW: "Mashal" (H4912) encompasses proverb, parable, and taunt-song — a form of wisdom literature weaponized as prophetic judgment speech. The five "woe" (hoy) oracles that follow (vv. 6, 9, 12, 15, 19) form a structured quintet of covenant curses against imperial hubris. HISTORICAL: The rhetorical device of conquered nations taking up a taunt against their oppressor is attested in ANE treaty literature and in Assyrian and Babylonian royal inscriptions where the tables are turned upon the boasting conqueror.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Will not your creditors rise suddenly, and those who make you tremble awake? Then you will become plunder for them.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

words.g: from blood of man or an animal

verses.flow: Because you have plundered many nations, all the rest of the peoples shall plunder you — on account of human blood and violence to the land, to the city, and to all who dwell in it.

words.g: from the hollow hand or palm

verses.flow: Woe to him who gets evil gain (betza) for his house, to set his nest on high, to be delivered from the hand of calamity!
verses.note: HEBREW: "Betza" (H1215) means unjust gain, profit obtained by violence or extortion — it carries the sense of cutting or tearing away what belongs to another. THEOLOGICAL: The image of building one's "nest on high" echoes the pride of Edom (Obad 4) and ultimately the Babel tradition (Gen 11) — the human drive to secure oneself beyond divine reach. Security obtained by exploitation is condemned as self-destruction rather than salvation.

verses.flow: You have devised shame for your house by cutting off many peoples; you have forfeited your own life.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: For the stone will cry out from the wall, and the beam from the woodwork will answer it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The personification of stone and timber as witnesses against injustice reflects the ANE concept of creation as a moral order that testifies. DIVINE_COUNCIL: In the broader OT worldview, the created order serves as a witness to covenant fidelity or faithlessness (cf. Deut 32:1, where heaven and earth are called as witnesses). The "crying stone" anticipates Jesus's statement that "the stones will cry out" (Luke 19:40) in a context of failed recognition of the visitation of God.

words.g: in/with a city in the widest sense
in/with blood of man or an animal

words.m: conjunction + verb polel sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Woe to him who builds a city with bloodshed and founds a town with iniquity!

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: Behold, is it not from YHWH of Hosts (Tsvaot) that peoples labor only for the fire, and nations weary themselves for nothing?
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The title "YHWH of Hosts" (YHWH Tsvaot, H6635) designates YHWH as commander of the heavenly armies — the divine council and angelic warriors — asserting his sovereignty over all empires. THEOLOGICAL: The irony is devastating: the Chaldean empire's vast labor and conquest, accomplished through violence, serves only to feed fire — its works are ultimately consumed. Human imperial glory is rendered vain before YHWH's cosmic sovereignty.

words.g: to fill or be full of
a sea or large body of water

verses.flow: For the earth shall be filled with the knowledge of the glory of YHWH, as the waters cover the sea.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This verse is nearly identical to Num 14:21 and echoes Isa 11:9 — a triad of texts affirming the eschatological filling of creation with divine glory. THEOLOGICAL: "Knowledge of the glory of YHWH" (da'at kavod YHWH) is not merely intellectual awareness but experiential, transformative recognition of the divine presence. In NT perspective this is inaugurated through the incarnation (John 1:14; 2 Cor 4:6) and consummated in the new creation (Rev 21:23). This verse stands at the structural and theological center of the five woe-oracles, serving as the horizon toward which all history moves.

verses.flow: Woe to him who makes his neighbor drink — you pour out your wrath and make them drunk as well — in order to gaze on their nakedness!

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: You shall be filled with shame instead of glory. Drink, you yourself, and be exposed! The cup in the right hand of YHWH shall come around to you, and utter shame shall come upon your glory.

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

words.g: from blood of man or an animal

verses.flow: For the violence done to Lebanon shall overwhelm you, and the destruction of the beasts shall terrify you — on account of human blood and violence to the land, to the city, and to all who dwell in it.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: What profit is an idol when its maker has shaped it — a cast image and a teacher of lies? For the one who fashions its form trusts in his own handiwork, making mute idols.
verses.note: HEBREW: The idol is called a "teacher of lies" (moreh sheker) — a biting irony since the word moreh (teacher, H4175) shares a root with Torah. False gods offer counterfeit instruction that corrupts rather than illuminates. THEOLOGICAL: The futility of idolatry is a major prophetic theme (Isa 44:9-20; Jer 10:1-16); here the craftsman's self-deception is highlighted — he trusts the product of his own hands, a circular and closed system of meaning that excludes the living God.

words.m: verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Woe to him who says to wood: "Awake!" to silent stone: "Arise!" Can this one give instruction? Behold, it is overlaid with gold and silver, and there is no breath (ruach) at all within it.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ruach" (H7307) — breath, wind, spirit — is absent from the idol, marking its fundamental lifelessness in contrast to YHWH who breathed life into Adam (Gen 2:7) and whose ruach animates all creation (Gen 1:2). The idol's gold and silver overlay represents superficial glory masking inner death — an inversion of YHWH's glory which is inherent and life-giving.

verses.flow: But YHWH is in his holy temple; let all the earth be silent (hush) before him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The concluding verse pivots from the futility of idols (v. 19) to the enthronement of the living YHWH in his heavenly temple — a cosmic contrast. "Let all the earth be silent (hush) before him" uses the Hebrew has, a word of solemn hushing, found also in Zeph 1:7 and Zech 2:13, marking a moment of awestruck reverence before divine action. DIVINE_COUNCIL: YHWH's "holy temple" here likely denotes the heavenly sanctuary where the divine council assembles (cf. Ps 82:1; Isa 6:1), not merely the Jerusalem temple — his sovereignty encompasses and transcends all earthly thrones addressed in the woe-oracles. TYPOLOGY: This verse serves as the theological bridge into Habakkuk's theophany hymn (ch. 3), where YHWH marches forth from his heavenly court as the Divine Warrior to vindicate his people.

words.g: the a prophet or inspired man

verses.flow: A prayer of Habakkuk the prophet, according to Shigionoth (shigyonot).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shigionoth" (pl. of shiggayon, H7692) likely denotes a lyrical poem of intense or impassioned style, possibly connected to wandering or irregular meter. The singular form appears in Psalm 7:1. That Habakkuk's lament culminates in a formal liturgical psalm signals the book's careful literary architecture. STRUCTURE: Chapter 3 functions as a theophanic hymn, drawing on ancient Exodus and Sinai traditions while bringing the book to a doxological resolution after the prophet's anguished dialogue with YHWH in chapters 1-2.

words.m: verb piel imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: O YHWH, I have heard your report (shemu'atkha), I stand in awe, O YHWH, of your work. In the midst of the years revive it; in the midst of the years make it known; in wrath (rogez) remember compassion (rahamim).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The prophet's petition "in wrath remember compassion (rahamim)" reflects the covenantal tension at the heart of the book: divine justice against Babylon must not extinguish Israel. "Rahamim" (H7356) derives from "rehem" (womb), conveying the deep, maternal tenderness of covenant love (hesed). This verse echoes the intercessory tradition of Moses at Sinai (Exod 32:11-13) and anticipates NT themes of mercy triumphing over judgment (James 2:13).

words.g: from a mountain or range of hills
to fill or be full of

verses.flow: God (Eloah) came from Teman, and the Holy One from Mount Paran. Selah. His splendor (hod) covered the heavens, and the earth was full of his praise (tehillah).
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The theophany originating from Teman and Paran deliberately echoes the Song of Moses (Deut 33:2) and Judges 5:4-5, placing this vision within the ancient tradition of YHWH as the Divine Warrior marching from the southern wilderness. "Eloah" (H433), the singular form rarely used outside Job and poetry, lends archaic, solemn weight. "Selah" (selah, H5542) likely signals a musical interlude or liturgical pause, appearing three times in this chapter. HISTORICAL: Teman was a region of Edom (southern Transjordan), and Paran a wilderness south of Canaan near Sinai, anchoring the theophany in the Exodus geography.

verses.flow: His brightness was like the light; rays (qarnayim) flashed from his hand, and there was the hiding of his power ('oz).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Qarnayim" (H7161) means literally "horns" or "rays," the same word used in Exod 34:29-30 where Moses' face "shone" (or "sprouted horns") after encountering YHWH. The ambiguity between radiant light and horns of power is intentional — both convey overwhelming divine energy emanating from YHWH's hands. THEOLOGICAL: The paradox of veiled power ("hiding of his power") suggests that the full force of YHWH's glory is necessarily concealed for creation to endure — a theme developed in Exod 33:20-23.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Before him went pestilence (deber), and plague (reshef) went forth at his feet.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Deber" (H1698, pestilence) and "reshef" (H7565, plague or fiery bolt) may function here not merely as natural phenomena but as divine attendants — members of YHWH's heavenly retinue — personified as warriors flanking the Divine King in procession. Reshef is attested in Ugaritic and Canaanite texts as a god of plague and war, here subordinated to YHWH's sovereignty. This reflects the divine council framework in which pagan deities are reframed as servants of YHWH. (Ps 91:5-6, Deut 32:24)

words.m: conjunction + verb polel sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb hithpolel sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: He stood and measured the earth; he looked and shook the nations; the everlasting mountains were shattered, the ancient hills sank low. His are the everlasting ways (halikhot 'olam).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Halikhot 'olam" — literally "ways of eternity" or "his are the eternal goings." The word "halikhot" (H1979) shares a root with "halakh" (to walk/go), emphasizing YHWH's active, purposeful movement through history. THEOLOGICAL: The shattering of everlasting mountains underscores that even the most permanent features of creation are subordinate to YHWH's sovereignty. This language anticipates apocalyptic texts where cosmic upheaval attends divine judgment (Rev 6:14, 16:20).

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: I saw the tents of Cushan in affliction ('aven); the curtains of the land of Midian trembled.

words.g: not + in/with a stream (including the sea
in/with a sea or large body of water

verses.flow: Was YHWH enraged against the rivers? Was your anger against the rivers, or your wrath against the sea, that you rode upon your horses, upon your chariot of salvation (yeshu'ah)?
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The rhetorical questions addressed to YHWH allude to the ancient Canaanite myth in which Baal defeats Yam (the sea deity) and Nahar (the rivers). Habakkuk deliberately invokes this mythological backdrop to assert YHWH's supremacy — it is not the rivers or sea that are YHWH's opponents, but the nations. The "chariot of salvation (yeshu'ah)" frames YHWH as the true Divine Warrior whose battles are redemptive in purpose. (Ps 74:13-14, Isa 51:9-10)

verses.flow: You stripped bare your bow; you called for many arrows. Selah. You split the earth with rivers.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix

words.g: a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: The mountains saw you and writhed; the raging of waters swept past; the deep (tehom) gave forth its voice, it lifted its hands on high.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Tehom" (H8415), the deep or abyss, echoes the primordial waters of Gen 1:2 and the cosmic sea of ANE cosmology. Its personified "voice" and upraised "hands" continue the mythological imagery of a defeated cosmic enemy paying homage to YHWH. HISTORICAL: This participates in a widespread ANE motif of sea-chaos subdued by the storm deity, here transformed into YHWH's historical acts at the Red Sea and Jordan River crossing.

verses.flow: The sun and moon stood still in their exalted dwelling; at the light of your arrows as they sped, at the flash of your glittering spear.

words.g: in/with strictly froth at the mouth

verses.flow: In indignation you marched through the earth; in anger you trampled the nations.

You went forth for the salvation (yesha') of your people, for the salvation of your anointed one (meshiakh). You crushed the head of the house of the wicked, laying him bare from foundation to neck. Selah.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "You went forth for the salvation of your anointed one (meshiakh)" is among the most explicitly messianic lines in the Twelve. "Meshiakh" (H4899) — the Anointed One — here refers to Israel's anointed king (Davidic line), but the language is paradigmatic for the ultimate deliverer. The crushing of the head of the wicked house may evoke Gen 3:15 (the protevangelium) and points forward to Christ's victory over the powers of evil (Col 2:15, Rev 19:11-16). THEOLOGICAL: "Yesha'" (salvation, H3468) shares a root with the name Yeshua (Jesus), making the verse's connection to NT soteriology linguistically direct.

words.m: preposition + verb hiphil infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: You pierced with his own shafts the head of his warriors, who stormed out to scatter me, whose rejoicing was as if to devour the poor in secret.

words.g: in/with a sea or large body of water

verses.flow: You trampled the sea with your horses, the surging of mighty waters.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing
verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: I heard, and my belly trembled; my lips quivered at the sound; rottenness entered into my bones; my legs trembled beneath me. I will quietly wait for the day of distress (tsarah) to come upon the people who invade us.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The prophet's visceral physical response — trembling belly, quivering lips, rottenness in bones — mirrors the reaction of Isaiah (Isa 6:5) and Ezekiel (Ezek 1:28) to divine encounter and revelation. Yet Habakkuk resolves not to flee but to "quietly wait" (nua'h), an act of radical trust. This verse is the emotional pivot of the chapter, connecting the terror of theophanic vision with the serene trust of verses 17-19. (Isa 30:15)

words.g: to break forth as a bud
a collective name for a flock

verses.flow: Though the fig tree should not blossom, and there be no fruit on the vines; though the produce of the olive fail, and the fields yield no food; though the flock be cut off from the fold, and there be no herd in the stalls —
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The conditional catalogue of agricultural failures — figs, vines, olives, grain, flocks, herds — represents the complete collapse of the covenant blessings of the land (Deut 28:15-24). To praise YHWH under such conditions is to transcend a transactional view of covenant relationship. HISTORICAL: Such agricultural devastation would have been the immediate consequence of Babylonian invasion and siege, giving the "though" clauses urgent, literal weight for Habakkuk's audience.

words.g: in/with gods in the ordinary sense

verses.flow: yet I will rejoice ('agilah) in YHWH; I will exult in the God of my salvation (Elohe yish'i).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "'Agilah" (I will rejoice, from H1523, gul/gil) connotes spinning or leaping with joy — an embodied, exuberant delight that stands in stark contrast to the physical trembling of v. 16. This is not stoic endurance but triumphant faith. "Elohe yish'i" (God of my salvation) echoes Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:47, "God my Savior"), illustrating the deep continuity between Habakkuk's faith and NT confessional joy. (Rom 5:2-3, Phil 4:4)

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

words.g: as/like a doe or female deer

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: YHWH the Lord (Adonai) is my strength (heli); he makes my feet like the feet of a deer; he makes me tread upon my high places. To the choirmaster (lamnatse'ah): with stringed instruments (bineginotai).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Heli" (my strength, from H2428, hayil) denotes vigor, valor, and military might — YHWH is the prophet's warrior-strength. The deer-feet imagery (cf. Ps 18:33/2 Sam 22:34) evokes both sure-footedness in dangerous terrain and swift, nimble movement. "Lamnatse'ah bineginotai" — "to the choirmaster, upon my stringed instruments" — reveals this was a living liturgical text, composed for congregational worship. STRUCTURE: The closing liturgical inscription forms a bracket with the opening "Shigionoth" in v. 1, confirming the chapter's function as a formal temple hymn embedded within the prophetic book. The book thus ends not with prediction but with praise — faith singing in the dark. (Ps 18:33, 2 Sam 22:34)
